Title (Units): COMP7025 Artificial Intelligence for Digital Transformation (3,2,1) 
 
Course Aims: To help students gain understanding of artificial intelligence (AI) and generative AI 
as essential tools in pursuing the digital transformation journey, through the 
discussion of AI basics, applications, and tools with use cases in various areas of an 
enterprise including sales and marketing, customer service, operations, risk
source: COMP7025 Artificial Intelligence for Digital Transformation (2025.09).pdf

text:
nd marketing, customer service, operations, risk 
management and other support functions. While applications of modern AI 
technologies and AI -generated content in different sectors will be explored, those 
for the banking industry will be highlighted. Students will also look at the issues and 
challenges of adopting AI technologies and generative AI methods in addition to 
their benefits and learn the implementation aspect of digital transformation. This
source: COMP7025 Artificial Intelligence for Digital Transformation (2025.09).pdf

text:
mentation aspect of digital transformation. This 
course is designed to prepare students to take part in digital transformation projects 
involving AI and generative AI technologies in different roles such as a business 
user, solution analyst, project manager, and AI solution provider. 
 
 
Prerequisite: Nil 
 
 
Course Intended Learning Outcomes (CILOs): 
Upon successful completion of this course, students should be able to: 
 
No. Course Intended Learning Outcomes (CILOs) 
 Knowledge
source: COMP7025 Artificial Intelligence for Digital Transformation (2025.09).pdf

e Intended Learning Outcomes (CILOs)

2 Describe the essence of digital transformation concepts and approach, and organizational readiness 
for adopting AI technologies 
3 Relate the real-life use cases and the related AI solutions 
4 Recognize the ethical, compliance, and potential risks associated with generative AI

source: COMP7025 Artificial Intelligence for Digital Transformation (2025.09).pdf

text:

6 Analyze and plan the readiness to adopt AI technologies in a digital transformation journey 
7 Identify and recommend AI solutions for business transformation 
8 Implement effective prompt engineering to utilize generative AI

Title (Units): COMP7095 Big Data Management (3,2,1) 
 
Course Aims: To introduce the fundamental issues of big data management; To learn the 
numeracy skills and the latest techniques for big data management and processing; 
To conduct application case studies to show how data management techniques 
support large-scale data processing. 
 
Prerequisite: Basic Knowledge in Database Concepts, Scripting and Programming. 
 
Course Intended Learning Outcomes (CILOs):

g. 
 
Course Intended Learning Outcomes (CILOs): 
Upon successful completion of this course, students should be able to: 
 
No. Course Intended Learning Outcomes (CILOs) 
 Knowledge 
1 Identify big data management problems and applications. 
2 Describe data management framework and methods. 
3 Describe applicability of data management applications. 
 Professional Skill 
4 Suggest appropriate solutions and tools to data management problems. 
5 Analyze data management algorithms and techniques.

alyze data management algorithms and techniques. 
 Attitude 
6 Work as a team to solve challenging data management problems. 
 
Calendar Description: This course aims to introduce fundamental issues of big data management and the 
common data management techniques and tools including streaming and I/O 
efficient algorithms, distributed computing, and NoSQL storage and processing. 
In addition, advanced topics and an in-depth case study of Apache Spark for

s and an in-depth case study of Apache Spark for 
various applications on streaming, SQL, and graphs will also be covered. 
Furthermore, the potential applications of big data management to several areas 
such as business, finance, and so forth, are shown via examples and practices. 
 
Teaching and Learning Activities (TLAs): 
 
CILOs Type of TLA 
1-3 Students will learn the concepts and algorithms from lectures.

learn the concepts and algorithms from lectures. 
4-5 Students will learn the big data management tools and techniques via tutorials, assignments, 
and guided laboratory. 
4-6 Students will work on a group project to gain hands-on experience of big data management.

COMP7115 
Digital Experience Design
Course Overview
1# commercialize#designthinking #digital #innovation#UX #UI

Topics to be covered
• Introduction to Digital Experience Design
• User-centered Design Process and Methods
• UI/UX
• Human-Computer Interaction
• Design Thinking
• Technologies for Digital Experience
• Human-AI Interaction
• From Design to Business Implementation - commercialize
Course Instructors
• Part A - Dr. Kristen LI 
• Part B – Mr. Donald CHAN (Co-founder & Senior Advisor, Cherrypicks)
2

Teaching & Learning Activities
• Lecture, workshop, lab
• Company Visit
• Teamwork

Assessments
▪ Workshop exercises and Individual Assignments (30%)
▪ Group Project (30%)
▪ Examination (40%)
In order to pass a course
▪ At least 30% of the final examination mark, and
▪ At least 35% of the overall mark
Group Project assessment consists of individual performance and group performance

Title (Units): COMP7520 Foundations of Management in the IT Context (3,3,0) 
 
Course Aims: This course aims to provide the foundation concepts of information technology 
management and its impacts on organizations. By studying this course, students 
will develop the knowledge and skills to effectively manage IT resources and 
enable digital transformation to meet business needs. Students will explore topics 
including strategic planning, digital business models, project management, and

digital business models, project management, and 
emerging technologies. 
 
Prerequisite: Nil 
 
Course Intended Learning Outcomes (CILOs): 
Upon successful completion of this course, students should be able to: 
 
No. Course Intended Learning Outcomes (CILOs) 
 Knowledge 
1 Explain the concepts including strategic plan, outsourcing, IT service management, and legal and 
ethical issues 
2 Explain the concepts of process management, and digital business models for digital transformation

gital business models for digital transformation 
3 Describe the emerging technologies, digital marketing, and cybersecurity management issues 
 Professional Skill 
4 Apply project management techniques for IT projects 
5 Prepare capital budgeting 
6 Apply IT management techniques to solve real world problems 
 
Calendar Description: This course provides the foundation concepts in managing IT resources and 
enabling digital transformation to meet business needs.

digital transformation to meet business needs. 
 
Teaching and Learning Activities (TLAs): 
 
CILOs Type of TLA 
1-3 Students will attend lectures for how IT could be aligned with business objectives. Students 
will be introduced with outsourcing practices, IT service management, and legal and ethical 
issues. Students will also learn the basic concepts for digital transformation, emerging 
technologies, digital marketing, and cybersecurity management issues.

marketing, and cybersecurity management issues. 
4-6 Students will conduct exercises on project management techniques, capital budgeting, and 
applying IT management techniques to solve real world problems. 
4-6 Students will work on a group project to analyze the problems of an organization, propose 
IT planning for it, and present the report orally. A test will assess the students’ 
understanding on the concepts covered in the course.

Comp7520
Foundations of Management in the IT 
Context
Course Introduction

Course Objectives
Provide the foundation concepts of information technology 
management and its impacts on organizations. 
Students will develop the knowledge and skills to effectively manage 
IT resources and enable digital transformation to meet business 
needs. 
Students will explore topics including strategic planning, digital 
business models, project management, and emerging technologies.

Learning Outcomes
No. Course Intended Learning Outcomes (CILOs)
Knowledge
1 Explain the concepts including strategic plan, outsourcing, IT service management, and legal 
and ethical issues
2 Explain the concepts of process management, and digital business models for digital 
transformation
3 Describe the emerging technologies, digital marketing, and cybersecurity management issues
Professional Skill
4 Apply project management techniques for IT projects
5 Prepare capital budgeting

iques for IT projects
5 Prepare capital budgeting
6 Apply IT management techniques to solve real world problems

Course Contents
1. Organization Issues and Principles of Management
2. Digital Transformation for Business Operations
3. IT Service and Project Management
4. Digital Marketing
5. Emerging Technologies
6. Cybersecurity Management
7. Legal and Ethical Aspects of Digital Transformation

Assessment Methods
Continuous Assessment 40% 
◦ Individual assignment (15%)
◦ Test (10%)
◦ Group Project and Presentation (10%) 
◦ Participation (5%)
Examination 60%
Important: To pass a course, students should receive at 
least 30 marks in the final examination. AND, overall mark 
of the course should not be less than 35%.

Avoid Plagiarism
"The University staunchly upholds the principles of academic integrity. 
As one part of HKBU’s effort to prevent plagiarism, the software 
Turnitin is used to compare all assignments against multiple sources 
whenever appropriate. A report on each assignment is generated that 
includes a percentage similarity and links to specific similar sources. 
Turnitin does not conclusively prove whether or not an assignment is 
plagiarized – the faculty will make this determination."

ized – the faculty will make this determination."

1 
 
Principles for the Use of Generative AI Tools 
in Teaching and Learning, and Assessment 
 (Approved by the Senate in June 2023) 
 
1. Introduction 
 
This document lays out the University’s principles for the use of generative AI tools in teaching 
and learning, and assessments. Generative AI is the part of Artificial Intelligence that leverages 
deep learning models to generate human -like content, includi ng audio, code, images, text,

e content, includi ng audio, code, images, text, 
simulations, 3D objects, and videos. Generative AI draws on data and/or the internet to generate 
texts. It can also generate new and unexpected outputs in response to varied and complex 
prompts (e.g., languages, instructions, questions). The type and function of generative AI tools 
have been growing rapidly. Generative AI tools can perform a growing list of tasks such as text

can perform a growing list of tasks such as text 
generation, idea generation, data analysis and evaluation, text summarization, translation, 
chatbot and conversational AI, sentiment analysis, text classification, paraphrasing, code 
generation, image generation, image captioning, music generation, and name entity recognition. 
The potential of the technology to transform teaching and learning is enormous, m aking it

rm teaching and learning is enormous, m aking it 
necessary for instructors and students to explore how to work with generative AI tools that can 
have multifunctionality and engage in a multiplicity of tasks. 
 
The implications of using generative AI tools in assessed assignment tasks, as with any tools, 
need to be carefully considered given that these tasks are intended to demonstrate the extent to 
which students have mastered Course Intended Learning Outcomes (CILOs) and count towards

nded Learning Outcomes (CILOs) and count towards 
students’ final grades. 
 
It is important to note that these principles are created to address the use of generative AI tools 
within the existing university teaching and learning ecosystem. Given the transformative 
effects of generative AI tools, there will need to be fundamental changes to teaching, learning 
and assessment across the higher education field. 
 
 
2. General Guiding Principles

tion field. 
 
 
2. General Guiding Principles 
 
1) AI- Empowering: to embrace generative AI and empower students and instructors to 
realize the potential of generative AI tools as part of the university’s knowledge 
creation and teaching and learning ecosystem 
 
2) AI-Critical: to encourage healthy, informed scepticism of generative AI and develop 
students’ and instructors’ critical thinking and awareness of the strengths, drawbacks 
and limitations of generative AI tools

wbacks 
and limitations of generative AI tools 
 
3) AI-Ethical: to maintain academic integrity, transparency and the ethical use of 
generative AI tools 
 
4) Build and sustain human uniqueness : to recognize the importance of students and 
instructors as unique individuals with lived experiences, knowledge, skills and 
attributes. Provide space and time for individuals to understand and develop 
themselves as unique individuals within the university teaching and learning 
ecosystem

4. Use of Generative AI in Assessment 
 
This section presents actions that instructors and/or students should take in relation to 
assessment in line with the guiding principles. The guiding principles are applicable to 
assignments requiring the generation of a range of output including but not limited to texts, 
codes, images, simulations, 3D objects, audio and videos, that can be produced with assistance 
from generative AI tools.

ced with assistance 
from generative AI tools. 
 
Given the proliferation of generative AI tools and their utility in teaching and learning, in 
alignment with our principles, the recommend ed approach is that students are allowed to 
perform the assessed assignment tasks with assistance from generative AI tools. Any such use 
must align with the University’s academic integrity guidelines for students on academic

ic integrity guidelines for students on academic 
integrity as well as the guidelines and instructions from the respective schools, faculties, 
departments and cours e instructors in order to ensure students are able to demonstrate 
achievement of PILOs and CILOs.

• Promote the use of generative AI tools to enhance students' original work in 
alignment with the CILOs and PILOs. 
• Design assessed assignment tasks that capitalize on the affordances of

demic integrity 
when using Generative AI tools.

5. Recommendations for Supporting Instructors and Students in Using 
Generative AI 
 
It is recognized that for the above uses to be actioned within the university community, there 
will need to be sustained development of instructors' and students’ AI literacy. 
 
Required action includes: 
 
• Establishing faculty and/or department-specific taskforces to establish, implement and

-specific taskforces to establish, implement and 
update specific guidelines for use of generative AI tools in teaching, learning and 
assessment to ensure students are able to achieve and demonstrate achievement of 
CILOs, PILOs, HKBU graduate attributes and Best Student Experience to s uit their 
disciplinary and student learning needs. 
 
Other possible actions might include: 
 
• Develop courses and co-curricular activities and events on learning in the digital age

vities and events on learning in the digital age 
and digital and AI literacy 
• Integrate digital and AI literacy into courses and co-curricular activities 
• Provide regular and sustained staff professional development activities and training on 
AI literacy 
• Develop a repository of AI -related teaching, learning and assessment resources and 
exemplars including examples of proper and improper use of generative AI tools

f proper and improper use of generative AI tools 
• Provide university subscription to Generative AI tools for instructors, administrative 
staff and student access 
• Create generative AI tools focused Teaching Development Grants (TDGs) 
• Organize forums, seminars and conferences on the effects of generative AI tools in 
education and the workforce 
• Update CILOs, PILOs to reflect the future needs of students and the community 
• Develop guidelines for the ethical use of generative AI tools

lines for the ethical use of generative AI tools 
• Establish reporting mechanisms of generative AI tools use 
• Consult the wider community and HKBU alumni on the uses of generative AI tools and 
the competences and literacies required of HKBU graduates 
 
6. Review and Update 
 
These principles should be reviewed and updated not less than once every two years, or more 
frequently as the need arises.

HONG KONG BAPTIST UNIVERSITY
POLICY FOR THE ASSESSMENT OF STUDENT LEARNING
1. Overview
1.1 Hong Kong Baptist University acknowledges that assessment is an integral
component of student learning experience . A sound assessment policy will help
articulate common guiding principles for all sub-degree, undergraduate, taught
postgraduate and research postgraduate programmes in the University , however
funded.
1.2 This policy specifies the basic requirements for the design, delivery and

e basic requirements for the design, delivery and
management of assessment and related activities. The University supports the
development of complementary requirements by individual Faculties/Schools and
programmes, provided that thos e are consistent with the guiding principles
highlighted in Section 3 below.The University recognizes and respects the diversity
of disciplinary characteristics and pedagogical needs across programmes and

stics and pedagogical needs across programmes and
Faculties/Schools, requiring different types of assessment and related activities. By
the same token, for some of the practices advocated in this policy , the
implementation details are left to therelevant academic units overseeing the course
in question (Faculty/School Boards, Programme Management Committees (PMCs),
or Departmental Assessment Committees (DACs)1, as the case may be).

sessment Committees (DACs)1, as the case may be).
1.3 Reference to international practice on student assessment has been made
in the process of drawing up this assessment policy.
1.4 For the purpose of this policy paper, the term “ Department” is used in the
generic sense to refer to the academic unitor equivalent offering a course, which is
responsible for teaching and thus assessment of a course, though in practice this

s assessment of a course, though in practice this
unit may carry other names such as Programme, Division or Academy. Accordingly,
the decision-making body for assessment matters of all the courses taught by a
Department is referred to in this policy paper generically as the “Departmental
Assessment Committee” (DAC), though in certainDepartments the remit and duties
of the DAC are taken up by the PMC. Respective roles and responsibilities of the

PMC. Respective roles and responsibilities of the
parties involved in this policy are set out in Section 13 below.
1.5 This policy paper applies to all forms of assessment, including but not limited
to face-to-face and online assessment tasks.
1 Renamed from the present Departmental Examination Committees.

2
2. Purposes of Assessment
2.1 Assessment serves a number of purposes, with the main ones being: –
For student learning:
l the provision of feedback to students to improve their learning
(formative);
l the overall assessment of student learning in a course, measuring what
students have achieved and not achieved (summative);
For teachers:
l the upholding of academic standards, differentiation of outstanding, good
and less well performing students , identification of student learning

ing students , identification of student learning
progress to facilitate class and curriculum planning (diagnostic); and
l the monitoring of student learning and performance as indicator s to
inform course effectiveness, providing useful information for teachers to
make self-improvement (evaluative).
3. Guiding Principles
3.1 Assessment should encourage and reinforce learning.
3.2 Assessment should align with learning outcomes at both the programme and
course levels.

outcomes at both the programme and
course levels.
3.3 Criterion-referenced assessment should be used in grading assessment
tasks.
3.4 Assessment must ensure that the desired academic standards of the
programmes are achieved.
3.5 Assessment should enable robust and fair judgments about student
performance and assessment practices must be fair and equitable to the students.
3.6 Assessment should be continuously reviewed and adjusted based on

ld be continuously reviewed and adjusted based on
student performance and feedback from various stakeholders.
3.7 Assessment should serve the purposes in paragraph 2.1.
3.8 Assessment should be authentic2 wherever appropriate, i.e. contextualized
in relevant settings.
2 Authentic assessment focuses on students using and applying knowledge and skills in real -life
settings. Ithelps students contextualize their learning and to see how real-life conditionsor situations

and to see how real-life conditionsor situations
affect their theoretical knowledge (Source from: https://teaching.unsw.edu.au/authentic-
assessment).

3
4. Approaches to Assessment
4.1 For every course, assessment tasks can take a variety of forms such as
essay, coursework assignment, oral presentation, written test/examination,
laboratory report, project, dissertation, as well as equivalent online assessment
tasks. For the purpose of student learning, each of these tasks, with the exception
of the one conducted at the end of the course, can be taken as both formative and

of the course, can be taken as both formative and
summative – formative in that it helps a student identify strengths and weaknesses
and thus ways for improving performance, and summative because its result
normally contributes tow ards the determination of the final grade for the course.
Purposes and intended outcomes of formative and summative assessment should
be made clear to students in the beginning of a course.
4.2 Assessment should gear to and be driven by the learning outcomes. Every

to and be driven by the learning outcomes. Every
assessment task in a course should aim at checking one or more of the learning
outcomes at the programme and course levels. The types of assessment should
also reflect the teaching and learning activities that are designed to achieve the
learning outcomes. Students should not be over-assessed or under-assessed. The
level of a course should be reflected by the rubrics and the types of assessment
adopted in the course.

nd the types of assessment
adopted in the course.
4.3 Assessment practices should enable teachers to measure students’ learning
progress at different points of a course and provide timely and effective feedback
to students before summative assessment is conducted at the end of the course.
To allow sufficient formative assessment, teachers are strongly advised to keep the
total weighting of all examination-type assessments in a course and any one

mination-type assessments in a course and any one
assessment task at or below 40% . However, programme s may adjust their
practices based on the assessment requirements stipulated by professional
accreditation bodies.
4.4 For formative purposes, the provision of timely and effective feedback to a
student about his/her performance in an assessment task is crucial to his/her
learning. This is further discussed in Section 8 below.

ng. This is further discussed in Section 8 below.
4.5 The number of assessment tasks and their scheduling should beappropriate
for the achievement of theCILOs of the course. In scheduling these tasks, the need
to give timely feedback after each task must be taken into consideration. The
weighting of an assessment task should reflect the importance of those CILOs
which are being assessed by that task, the extent to which such CILOs are
measured and the workload.

o which such CILOs are
measured and the workload.
4.6 For a course involving team teaching or with multiple teachers/sections
including academic and community partners , a coordinator must be appointed to
collate informati on from the different teachers. Similarly for the assessment
guidelines, as well as the weighting of each task, viz. how its result will contribute
to the final grade of the course.

4
5. Assessment Rubrics
5.1 Assessment rubrics should bedeveloped for all major assessment tasks and
be included in the assessment information to begiven out to the students. They are
meant for promoting student engagement in the teaching and learning process
through communicating to them at the outset the aims and expectations of
assessment.
5.2 Assessment rubrics may be generic or discipline/course specific. Therubrics
pertaining to an assessment task in a course should spell out inter alia

ment task in a course should spell out inter alia
l how the particular assessment task is aligned with the CILOs.
l the assessment criteria and standards, which must be based on the CILOs
being assessed.
6. Communication to Students
6.1 The establishment of assessment criteria and standards upfront and making
them transparent to students highlights the adoption of criterion referencing in
marking and grading assessments.

n referencing in
marking and grading assessments.
6.2 Information about the assessment tasks in a course, such as any submission
procedures and deadlines, penalty for late submission (if applicable), should be
given to students as soon as practicable within the first two weeks of classes.
6.3 The teacher of a course may choose togive, as soon as teaching has started,
all the assessment rubrics of the course, for those students who may wish to have

e course, for those students who may wish to have
early access to such information (e.g. uploading the rubrics to a course room ofthe
University’s Learning Management System).
6.4 Assessment tasks and their weightings should not be changed afterteaching
in a course has started and the students have been notified. Under exceptional
circumstances when a change cannot be avoided, prior written approval from the
Head of Department , who would consult with the Programme Director , must be

uld consult with the Programme Director , must be
obtained as part of the quality assurance protocol;and the approved change should
be reported to the Faculty Board and QAC for record, and communicated to the
affected students promptly.
7. Grade Descriptors
7.1 The University has established a set of generic grade descriptors for
undergraduate courses and another set fortaught postgraduate courses, and these
are set out in detail in the University Student Handbook at

t in detail in the University Student Handbook at
https://ar.hkbu.edu.hk/student-services/useful-information/publications. (For
undergraduate programmes, please select General Regulations > Undergraduate
Degree Programme s > Assessment Grading System . Fo r taught postgraduate

5
programmes, please select General Regulations > Professional Doctorate Degree/
Taught Postgraduate Degree/ Diploma/ Certificate Programmes > Assessment
Grading System)
7.2 Grading/Assessment rubrics at the Faculty/School level as well as the
course/programme level may be developed, together with course -specific grade
descriptors which should be consistent with the university -wide grade descriptors
set out under paragraph 7.1.
8. Giving Feedback to Students

der paragraph 7.1.
8. Giving Feedback to Students
8.1 Constructive and effective feedback, be it in the form of verbal, written or
online mode, must be provided in a timely manner to a student foreach assessment
task he/she has completed in a course . For the final assessment task or the
semester-end written examination in a course, teachers are encouraged to return
qualitative feedback to students, either collectively or individually, to facilitate their

collectively or individually, to facilitate their
learning in future courses. As timeliness is crucial, the work submitted by a student
for an assessment task should be marked/graded and returned to the student with
appropriate feedback as soon as practicable and in any case no later than three
weeks after the deadline for submission. For some assessment tasks, the re may
be good reasons for requiring a longer timeframe, and prior approval for extension

onger timeframe, and prior approval for extension
should be sought from the Head of Department.
8.2 In order to be constructive and formative, feedback should be aimed at
facilitating a student’s learning and assisting him/her to prepare for subsequent
assessment tasks and improve his/her performance. The forms of assessment
should enable such feedback. Feedback must also be linked to the assessment
criteria and rubrics.
8.3 Feedback can be delivered in many ways including but not limited to

livered in many ways including but not limited to
individual oral feedback, group discussion, individual written comments, feedback
sheets, peer comments, descriptive annotations and suggested answers.
9. Moderation of Assessment
9.1 Both continuous assessment and the last assessment task may have a
heavy weighting in the determination of the final grade for the course. Given the
University’s emphasis on ongoing assessment,every assessment task may benefit

oing assessment,every assessment task may benefit
from peer discussion and review . In particular, those tasks carrying a heavy
weighting toward the final grade should be moderated.
9.2 The purpose of moderation is to enhance the validity of assessment and
reliability between different assessors, tasks or as sessment tracks, achieve
standardization of grading, align interpretations of assessment rubrics, ensure
academic standards and differentiate different levels of standards, and address

ntiate different levels of standards, and address
marginal and/or extreme cases.

6
9.3 As the final arbiter on matters related to assessment and other continuous
assessment, th e DAC should have inter alia the authority to approve the
assessment tasks set by the course teachers and adjust the grades they submit,
when there are justifications for doing so. Efforts should be made to improve t he
rigour of the moderation process for every course.

sessment guidelines
communicated to the students.
l the assessment task(s) can provide a fair assessment of the students which is

of the assessment tasks and the time allowed for completing them.
l there are succinct assessment criteria and grade descriptors.
9.4 As a matter of good practice, the course coordinator in charge ofdeveloping
the common assessment tasks and rubrics should do so in consultation with all

and rubrics should do so in consultation with all
other teachers of the same course. The common assessment rubric s should be

9.5 The final grades of the students in a course should be reported to the relevant
DAC, which has a role in monitoring these grades and checking whether there are
apparent anomalies in any of them. Exactly what would constitute such an anomaly

em. Exactly what would constitute such an anomaly
is a matter which depends on inter alia the discipline area as well as the nature of a
course, and it is therefore best left to the academic judgment of the DAC concerned.
With that being said and as an alternative method of moderation , th e respective
course coordinator/ teacher should monitor student performance in different
assessment tasks and detect any anomalies. This arrangement c an also increase

ny anomalies. This arrangement c an also increase
validity and reliability of various assessment tasks within a course.
9.6 If and when there is an apparent anomaly, the course teacher/coordinator
should be invited to give an explanation tothe DAC, making reference to inter alia the
published assessment criteria and standards, the application of such criteria and
standards, the assessment tasks, and the grading or assessment rubrics if applicable.

the grading or assessment rubrics if applicable.
In light of this explanation, theDAC will decide whether there is a need for anyfollow-
up action which may lead to the adjustment of some of the grades.
9.7 There are many possibilities for this follow-up action of moderation, including
for instance double marking by another teacher for certain assessment task (s), a
sample of the scripts, or only those cases considered to be marginal/borderline

those cases considered to be marginal/borderline
between two grades. Not only for moderation purposes, double marking is an
internationally recognized practice that can increase the extent to which the
assessment outcomes are fair and valid, especially for open -ended and less
structured assessments. The University encourages a wider application of double
marking, while recognizing that for some courses there are constraints due to staff
workload and tight deadlines.

raints due to staff
workload and tight deadlines.
9.8 The DAC can choose the best course of action dependingon the gravity of the

7
anomaly and the practicalities surrounding a case. If so warranted by the gravity of
the situation, the DAC may decide to seek assistance from external peers in
conducting the double marking or pursuing other follow -up actions . The course
coordinator should be informed by theDAC in writing of any moderation to the course
grades.
9.9 For a small number of programmes, there is external moderation of
assessment. Departmental Academic Advisors (DAA) appointed by Departments will

emic Advisors (DAA) appointed by Departments will
also review samples of assessed student materials to ensure appropriate standards
and fair and consistent marking.
10. Retention of Assessed Materials
10.1 For the purpose of facilitating internal and/or external moderation, as well as
to allow for the need to conduct investigation in possible cases of student appeal
against the final grade and/or academic misconduct, the final examination scripts

cademic misconduct, the final examination scripts
of the students (and similar assessed materials when the en d-of-course
assessment takes a form different from written examination) are retained by the
Department of the course teacher/coordinator for a specified period of time. The
retention period is six months from the deadline for receiving student appeals
against the final grade.
10.2 For the purpose of documenting evidence of student learning for various

umenting evidence of student learning for various
needs such as requests from external review panels in accreditation and/or
Academic Consultation Panel (ACP) exercises, some selected samples of students’
assessed materials will be retained (in electronic formats) forlonger than sixmonths
and up to six years . Such student materials should be de -identified to maintain
anonymity of individual students.
10.3 The University’s guidelines on confidentiality and personal data privacy must

on confidentiality and personal data privacy must
be observed in handling all matters regarding the storage and disposal of these
assessed materials of the students.
11. Academic Integrity
11.1 The University upholds a high standard of academicintegrity and all students
have an obligation to understand and follow honest academic practices inpursuing
their studies in the University. As far as assessment is concerned, al l work

ity. As far as assessment is concerned, al l work
submitted for assessment by a student , irrespective of its mode of administration
and format , is accepted on the understanding that it is the student’s own effort
without falsification of any kind.
11.2 Any case of suspected academic misconduct will be actively investigated by
the University, in order to safeguard the integrity and credibility of its awards and to
ensure fairness for all students. Any substantiated breach of the established

ents. Any substantiated breach of the established
requirements for academic integrity – whether intentional or due to a failure to take

specific and serious form of academic misconduct.
Information about what constitutes plagiarism must be communicated clearly and
made readily available to all students at all times (https://ar.hkbu.edu.hk/quality-
assurance/university-policy-and-guidelines/avoiding-plagiarism). Students are also
informed that their written assessment tasks will be checkedagainst Internet-based
plagiarism software tools as stipulated by the University. In the context of such

pulated by the University. In the context of such
information the course teacher should, at the start of a course, provide guidance to
his/her students accordingly, drawing on any course -specific requirements as
appropriate.
11.5 Clear guidelines regarding academic integrityand what constitutesacademic
misconduct must be formulated and disseminated widely to students and staff .
Similarly for guidelines on the procedures f or the investigation of suspected

he procedures f or the investigation of suspected
academic misconduct and the possible penalties. The AR should coordinate this
process of information dissemination by collating pertinent information from other
offices (such as the Graduate School) and providing a central point of access to
students and staff.
11.6 Course teachers should take care to minimize the opportunities foracademic
dishonesty, when designing assessment tasks and setting the assessment

gning assessment tasks and setting the assessment
topics/questions in such a task. In that connection, it is a good practice to refrain
from assigning too heavy a weight to any single assessment task of a course, as
well as to ensure that assessment topics/questions are regularly reviewed and to
avoid re -using the same or similar materials in recent examination papers or
assignments. More importantly, assessment tasks and questions/topics requiring

, assessment tasks and questions/topics requiring
knowledge recall are less preferred than those which aim at testing a student’s
higher-order cognitive abilities such as application, analysis, synthesis and
evaluation.

9
11.7 To enhance students’ awareness of academic integrity, all new students are
required to complete the Academic Integrity Online Tutorial (AIOT) in the beginning
of their study at HKBU. The AIOT provides students withinformation and strategies
that can be applied to avoid committing academic misconduct unintentionally. Upon
completion, students have to make a declaration, confirming their understanding of
academic integrity and that any acts of academic misconduct may result in serious

acts of academic misconduct may result in serious
consequences.
11.8 All members in the University have the obligation to respect ownership and
intellectual property rights. Materials produced by teachers, students and other
people involved in the course , including the recorded lectures , are protected by
copyright. One must not share, in any formats, such materials within and outside
the University for any purposes without permission and authorization from the

ses without permission and authorization from the
respective teacher/students/parties.
12. Mitigation and Review
12.1 A student who is prevented from completing an assessment task by
extenuating circumstances beyond his/her own control must be given the
opportunity to seek mitigation, in accordance with established procedural guidelines
(https://handbook.ar.hkbu.edu.hk/).
12.2 A student with Special Educational Needs can apply to the Academic

ecial Educational Needs can apply to the Academic
Registry/Graduate School for additional support or adjustments to assessment
which he/she may require. Without compromising the academic rigour of an
assessment task, such a request should be met.
12.3 For students who cannot complete and submit all the work for assessment
in time, an interim grade would usually be assigned to them during those
circumstances.
12.4 A student who believes that his/her final grade in a course is unfair or

that his/her final grade in a course is unfair or
inaccurate may request for a review, in accordance with clearly established
procedural guidelines (https://handbook.ar.hkbu.edu.hk/).
13. Roles and Responsibilities
13.1 The formulation and execution of this assessment policy, as well as
subjecting it to regular review in the light of experience, depends on the genuine
collaboration of many parties in general and the following in particular –
At University level:

he following in particular –
At University level:
(a) The Teaching and Learning Policy Committee (TLPC) is responsible for
developing and overseeing the implementation of th is institution-wide
assessment policy. Th is policy document provides guidance to students,

10
teachers, committees and assessment boards (or equivalent). The TLPC
should monitor the effective implementation, review the policy document where
appropriate and propose necessary remedial actions to the Senate from time
to time.
(b) Working closely with the TLPC, t he Quality Assurance Committee (QAC) is
responsible for ensuring compliance with the assessment policy when
academic units submit programme and course documents for its review as part
of the quality assurance process.

review as part
of the quality assurance process.
(c) The Academic Registry (AR), Graduate School (GS) and equivalent unit in the
School of Continuing Education, are responsible for implementing the academic
regulations which underpin the various practices set out in the institution-wide
assessment policy.
(d) The Centre for Holistic Teaching and Learning ( CHTL) is responsible for
assisting teachers to develop assessment rubrics and reviewing the

s to develop assessment rubrics and reviewing the
mechanism for systematic feedback. In the induction or related professional
development activities, the CHTL provides an overview of the assessment
policy to new and other in -service teachers to he lp them identify the major
assessment issues in the University.
At Faculty/School/AVA level:
(e) The Deans, who chair the respective Faculty/School boards, assume the
overall responsibility for the oversight of assessment methods and grading

r the oversight of assessment methods and grading
within their respective F aculties/Schools, as part of the quality assurance
mechanism for their programmes. Deans should e nsure that the Departments
comply with the institutional assessment policy.
At Department/Programme level:
(f) The Heads of Department are the “gatekeepers” who monitorcompliance at the
department level. They should oversee the supportive structures for individual

oversee the supportive structures for individual
Departments for the successful implementation of this policy . At the beginning
of each academic year, the Heads should remind all course teachers of the
salient points in this policy document.
(g) The PMC, or equivalent, should be formed for every programme. As the Chair
of a PMC, the Programme Director should be in charge of the design and
revision of programme curriculum and various student matters (such as

e curriculum and various student matters (such as
academic problems, progression and graduation). Under his/her guidance, the
PMC should have an assessment plan for the evaluation of learning outcomes
and student performance at the programme level, and oversee the
development of assessment rubrics. In some cases, a Head of Department can
also be Programme Director.
(h) The DAC sets general principles and guidelines for assessment at

neral principles and guidelines for assessment at
department/programme level. The DAC also approves the assessment tasks
set by the course teachers, including the overseas and community partners who
are involved in assessment, and adjusts the grades they submit if any
anomalies are detected . In many cases, the remit and duties of the DAC are
taken up by the PMC.

11
At Course/Individual level:
(i) Teachers have the responsibility for developing engaging pedagogies and
relevant assessment methods, including assessment rubrics, and for helping
students achieve the outcomes of the courses they teach. They are expected
to analyze and interpret the assessment data with the view to introducing
instructional changes in their courses. Based on the feedback from various
stakeholders, they should review and make appropriate adjustment to

should review and make appropriate adjustment to
assessment as deemed appropriate. Teachers who are course coordinators
should provide advice and ensure all teachers and external teaching partners,
both local and non-local, if any, in the course will observe this assessment
policy and the assessment arrangements and rubrics of the course.
(j) While bringing in useful practical experience to the curriculum, the part-time

tical experience to the curriculum, the part-time
teachers and external teaching partnersshould observe relevant guidelines and
regulations when assessing student performance in a course. They will be
subject to the same quality assurance control and standards applying to full-
time teachers.
(k) The students should t ake charge of their own learning and ensure that they
understand the aims , purposes and expectations of different types of

, purposes and expectations of different types of
assessment, and strive to perform to thebest of their ability. They must observe
the regulations for assessment andconduct themselves with academic honesty
and integrity at all times.
14. Review and Dissemination
14.1 As a matter of good practice, this policy should be kept under regular review
and updated in the light of experience with its implementation . Apart from posting

ence with its implementation . Apart from posting
this policy on the intranet and other websites within the University community, it will
be widely disseminated to all teachers at the onset of each academic year. This
policy will also be explained to all new students in the induction programme at the
beginning of each academic year.

HONG KONG BAPTIST UNIVERSITY
Strategy for Digital Learning and Teaching
(Approved by the Senate in September 2022)
1. Introduction
This document provides a general framework for the integration of pedagogy and technology into
learning and teaching at HKBU in all modes based on the principles of digital learning and teaching
(DLT) (Grant-Smith, et al, 2019). The strategic priorities and actions herein align closely with the

orities and actions herein align closely with the
directions and foci of HKBU’s relevant strategic documents, including Institutional Strategic Plan
2018 – 28, Planning Exercise Proposal 2022/23 – 2024/25 Triennium, Policy for the Assessment of
Student Learning, Learning and Teaching Strategy and Quality Assurance Framework.
2. Vision
To provide an inclusive, flexible, engaging, and transformative physical, hybrid, mixed and/or virtual

sformative physical, hybrid, mixed and/or virtual
learning and teaching environment to achieve the graduate attributes and future-readiness, as well as
for knowledge creation and transfer through advanced technologies.
3. Rationale
In using digital technology for learning and teaching, we:
● adhere to a learner-centred approach;
● cater for students with a variety of abilities, aspirations and backgrounds;

riety of abilities, aspirations and backgrounds; 
● integrate virtual and physical means to enhance teaching, learning, self-study, and collaboration
beyond limitations of distance and time; and
● strive for fit-for-purpose deployment of resources.
4. Scope
This strategy covers all educational opportunities at HKBU, including formal and informal curricular,
as well as co- and extra-curricular learning and teaching activities, on and off campus.

rning and teaching activities, on and off campus.
5. Benefits of Using Digital T echnology in Learning and Teaching
Digital technology can bring about benefits in learning and teaching, for example:
Providing broader accessibility – Without the constraints of fixed time and location, DLT enables
students' accessibility to diverse sources of knowledge, facilitates different forms of interaction, and
increases students' learning ownership (i.e., individualised learning) with quick and immediate

individualised learning) with quick and immediate
feedback.
Enhancing opportunities and quality of student learning – Cutting-edge education technology can
also diversify and amplify students’ collaborative learning experiences for better achievement of
learning outcomes. DLT also facilitates the design of novel e-pedagogical and assessment methods to
complement physical activities.
Developing core competencies in the digital era– Using technology in learning and teaching equips

Using technology in learning and teaching equips
students with essential technology-related skills, digital literacy, and digital ethics as workers, citizens
and lifelong learners in a digital society. It enables them to tackle unpredictable future challenges

2
brought by technological advancement.
Promoting evidence-based approach – Learning analytics advance an evidence-based approach for
programme and pedagogical development, assessment and quality assurance. Data on student learning
also supports research on learning and teaching, and informs policies and decisions.
6. Strategic Priorities
Underpinning this Strategy are four broad priorities at the University, and Programme and Course
levels:
At University level, we aim at:

ital resources by all students
Specific actions1:
- provide affordable, quality, and user-friendly DLT resourcesand related student support; and
- raise the University community’s awareness and understanding of students’ diverse learning
needs.
At Programme and Course levels, we aim at:
6.3 Using digital tools for diverse pedagogies, innovative programmes and course designs
Specific actions1:
- adopt digital tools to enhance the development of research-led curriculum, and experiential
learning;

omplement in-person activities
Specific actions1:
- adopt online activities and other blended initiatives to promote collaborative learning and
teaching with partners worldwide;
- strengthen virtual co-curricular and extra-curricular activities to connect students with their
counterparts worldwide; and
- implement exchange programmes that adopt physical and virtual components.
